summary: Cosmic Perspective is a completed lecture series held in Yerevan, Armenia in March 2025, led by curator and astrophysicist Jan Świerkoski. The masterclass explored how art helps humans engage with scientific concepts beyond sensory perception, from atoms to black holes, examining cognitive shifts similar to the Overview Effect. Participants learned strategies for creating successful art-science residencies and explored the work of Instytut B61, an international art-science collective. The single-day event attracted 74 on-site attendees and was conducted in Armenian and English, receiving a feedback score of 8/10.

pros: Strong speaker credentials with interdisciplinary research background in astrophysics and cultural studies
Clear thematic focus on art-science engagement grounded in cognitive theory (Cosmic Underground)
Established institutional research affiliation and past high-profile projects (Cosmic Underground)
cons: No connection to Yerevan cultural context or local audience needs—theme remains abstract and generic
Minimal local embedding: venue partnership only, no co-creation or involvement of Armenian cultural actors
Originality unclear: standard lecture format with references to past work, no novel methodology demonstrated
Research depth not traced to this event: no lecture-specific artifacts, bibliography, or process documentation

pros: Strong intellectual content with art-science integration focus
Completed event with documented audience (74 on-site) and feedback (8/10)
International expert (Jan Świerkoski) with credible background in art-science curation
cons: Zero economic sustainability framework - no revenue model or financial logic beyond budget item numbers
No employment dimension - single-person team with no paid or meaningful work opportunities addressed
No creative value chain integration - no distribution, market access, or commercial pathways
No financial innovation - standard grant-dependent single-event structure

pros: Bilingual delivery in Armenian and English with artifact-level evidence
Interactive hands-on component providing some participant engagement
Public-event format with documented attendance of 74 people
cons: No identification of marginalized target groups or mechanisms for their meaningful representation
Absence of accessibility provisions (physical, economic, digital) beyond generic 'public' label
No local community co-creation, participatory governance, or engagement structures
Limited cultural mediation or multicultural design beyond language provision

Missed opportunity to connect cosmic themes to Armenian astronomical heritage or local cultural context
